prompt:
I maintain the newsroom's media-library CLI and I'm ripping out the third guilty hand-rolled matcher this year — every one of them broke the moment a filename had a '(' or a '+' in it, because they translated globs to regexes by string-pasting. I want this done properly once, in a new file GlobRename.java (class GlobRename), against the acceptance tests already sitting in TestMain.java (`java TestMain.java` runs them; that file is off limits). Two static methods.

`Pattern compile(String glob)` — turn a glob into a java.util.regex.Pattern that matches an ENTIRE file name (we call .matches(), no partial hits). Rules: `*` is any run, including empty, of characters except `/`; `?` is exactly one character except `/`; a backslash makes the following character literal (so `\*` is a real asterisk — remember that's "\\*" in Java source); every other character is plain text no matter what it means to the regex engine — dots, parens, plus, pipe, all of it. Wrap literal runs with Pattern.quote or equivalent, and mind that a quoted island has to survive literal text like a backslash followed by 'E' — the tests feed it exactly that, four-backslashes-in-source style. Each `*` and `?` must be a capturing group, numbered left to right, so callers can pull the matched runs back out. A glob ending in a lone backslash is a caller bug: throw IllegalArgumentException.

`String rename(String name, String glob, String target)` — the planning half: if name doesn't fully match glob, return null; otherwise build the new name from target, where `{1}`, `{2}`, ... (digits between braces) splice in the corresponding wildcard captures and EVERYTHING else is plain text. Plain text means plain text: a `$` in the target is a dollar sign, a backslash is a backslash — Matcher replacement syntax must not leak through (Matcher.quoteReplacement is your friend if you go through the replace APIs). A brace that isn't a `{digits}` placeholder stays as-is. A placeholder pointing past the number of wildcards in the glob throws IllegalArgumentException. Captured text containing `$` or backslashes has to come through untouched too.

Stdlib only. The tests enumerate the escaping ladder cases we actually got burned by, so read them as the spec where my summary is loose.
